questions.question: If \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) are subsets of a given set, then which one of the following relations is not correct?
questions.solution: Option A: by absorption law, \(A\cup(A\cap B) = A \neq A\cup B\) in general. Options B (absorption), C and D (distributive laws) are all correct standard identities. Common mistake: confusing the absorption law \(A\cup(A\cap B)=A\) with \(A\cup B\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If a set \(A\) contains 3 elements and another set \(B\) contains 6 elements, then what is the minimum number of elements that \(A \cup B\) can have?
questions.solution: The minimum of \(|A\cup B|\) occurs when \(A \subseteq B\), giving \(|A\cup B| = |B| = 6\). The maximum is \(|A|+|B|=9\) when \(A\cap B=\phi\). Common mistake: choosing 3 (minimum of the two sets) instead of recognizing the larger set as the bound.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Consider the following statements: 1. \(A = \{1,3,5\}\) and \(B = \{2,4,7\}\) are equivalent sets. 2. \(A = \{1,5,9\}\) and \(B = \{1,5,5,9,9\}\) are equal sets. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.solution: Equivalent sets have the same cardinality: both \(A\) and \(B\) have 3 elements, so statement 1 is true. Equal sets contain the same elements; repeated elements in a set don't count multiple times, so \(B=\{1,5,9\}\) making statement 2 true. Common mistake: confusing 'equal' (same elements) with 'equivalent' (same size).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Consider the following statements: 1. The null set is a subset of every set. 2. Every set is a subset of itself. 3. If a set has 10 elements, then its power set will have 1024 elements. Which of the above statements are correct?
questions.solution: All three are correct: \(\phi \subseteq A\) for all \(A\); \(A \subseteq A\) always; and \(|P(A)| = 2^{10} = 1024\). These are fundamental properties of sets and power sets. Common mistake: doubting statement 1 or miscalculating \(2^{10}\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(R\) be a relation defined as \(xRy\) if and only if \(2x + 3y = 20\), where \(x, y \in \mathbb{N}\). How many elements of the form \((x,y)\) are there in \(R\)?
questions.solution: Solve \(2x+3y=20\) for \(x,y\in\mathbb{N}\) (positive integers): \(y=1\Rightarrow x=8.5\) (not integer); \(y=2\Rightarrow x=7\); \(y=4\Rightarrow x=4\); \(y=6\Rightarrow x=1\). So valid pairs are \((7,2),(4,4),(1,6)\), giving 3 elements. Common mistake: including \(y=0\) or non-integer solutions, or missing valid pairs.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(R\) be a relation from \(\mathbb{N}\) to \(\mathbb{N}\) defined by \(R = \{(x,y): x,y \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(x^{2} = y^{3}\}\). Which of the following are not correct? 1. \((x,x) \in R\) for all \(x \in \mathbb{N}\)  2. \((x,y)\in R \Rightarrow (y,x)\in R\)  3. \((x,y)\in R\) and \((y,z)\in R \Rightarrow (x,z)\in R\)
questions.solution: Statement 1 fails: \(x^{2}=x^{3}\) only for \(x=1\), not all \(x\in\mathbb{N}\). Statement 2 fails: \((1,1)\in R\) trivially, but \((64,16)\in R\) since \(64^{2}=4096=16^{3}\), yet \(16^{2}=256\neq 64^{3}\). Statement 3 also fails by counterexample. All three properties fail, so all are 'not correct'. Common mistake: assuming transitivity holds without verifying.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Consider the following: 1. \(A \cap B = A \cap C \Rightarrow B = C\)  2. \(A \cup B = A \cup C \Rightarrow B = C\). Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.solution: Both are false. Counterexample for 1: \(A=\{1,2\}, B=\{1,3\}, C=\{1,4\}\); \(A\cap B=\{1\}=A\cap C\) but \(B\neq C\). Similar counterexample works for 2. Intersection/union cancellation does not hold in general. Common mistake: treating set operations like algebraic equations where cancellation is valid.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: A set \(S\) contains \((2n+1)\) elements. There are 4096 subsets of \(S\) which contain at most \(n\) elements. What is \(n\) equal to?
questions.solution: By symmetry, subsets of size \(\leq n\) from a set of \(2n+1\) elements total exactly half of all subsets: \(\frac{2^{2n+1}}{2} = 2^{2n} = 4096 = 2^{12}\). So \(2n=12\), giving \(n=6\). Wait — re-checking: \(2^{2n}=4096\Rightarrow n=6\). But answer is A(5)? Let \(2n+1\) elements: total subsets \(=2^{2n+1}\), subsets of size \(\leq n\) \(=2^{2n+1}/2=2^{2n}=4096\), so \(n=6\). The correct answer is B(6). [Note: official answer is likely 6.]

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The Cartesian product \(A \times A\) has 16 elements among which are \((0,2)\) and \((1,3)\). Which of the following is/are correct? 1. It is possible to determine set \(A\). 2. \(A \times A\) contains the element \((3,2)\).
questions.solution: \(|A\times A|=16\Rightarrow |A|=4\). From \((0,2)\) and \((1,3)\), \(A\) contains \(0,1,2,3\). Since \(|A|=4\), \(A=\{0,1,2,3\}\) is uniquely determined. Then \((3,2)\in A\times A\). Common mistake: thinking \(A\) cannot be determined because we only see two elements of \(A\times A\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(A\) and \(B\) are two non-empty sets having 10 elements in common, then how many elements do \(A \times B\) and \(B \times A\) have in common?
questions.solution: \((A\times B)\cap(B\times A) = (A\cap B)\times(B\cap A) = (A\cap B)\times(A\cap B)\). Since \(|A\cap B|=10\), the number of common elements is \(10\times 10=100\). Common mistake: computing \(10+10=20\) or \(10\times 2=20\) instead of squaring.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: For each non-zero real number \(x\), let \(f(x) = \frac{x}{|x|}\). The range of \(f\) is
questions.optionB: a set consisting of only one element
questions.optionC: a set consisting of two elements
questions.optionD: a set consisting of infinitely many elements
questions.solution: \(f(x)=\frac{x}{|x|}=+1\) if \(x>0\) and \(-1\) if \(x<0\). So the range is \(\{-1,1\}\), a set with exactly two elements. Common mistake: thinking the range is \((-1,1)\) or confusing this with a continuous function.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(f(x) = \log_e\!\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)\), \(g(x) = \frac{3x+x^{3}}{1+3x^{2}}\) and \(g \circ f(t) = g(f(t))\), then what is \(g \circ f\!\left(\frac{e-1}{e+1}\right)\) equal to?
questions.solution: Note \(g(x)=\frac{3x+x^3}{1+3x^2}\) is the triple-angle tangent formula related to \(\tanh\). Key insight: \(f\!\left(\frac{e-1}{e+1}\right)=\log_e\!\left(\frac{1+\frac{e-1}{e+1}}{1-\frac{e-1}{e+1}}\right)=\log_e(e)=1\). Then \(g(f(t))=g(1)=\frac{3+1}{1+3}=\frac{4}{4}=1\)... re-evaluating with \(t=\frac{e-1}{e+1}\) gives \(f(t)=1\), \(g(1)=1\). Answer should be B. Common mistake: miscalculating \(f\) at the given point.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Consider the function \(f(x) = \frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+1}\), where \(x \in \mathbb{R}\). At what value of \(x\) does \(f(x)\) attain minimum value?
questions.solution: \(f(x)=1-\frac{2}{x^{2}+1}\) is minimized when \(\frac{2}{x^{2}+1}\) is maximized, i.e., when \(x^{2}+1\) is minimized. The minimum of \(x^{2}+1\) is 1, occurring at \(x=0\). Common mistake: differentiating and solving incorrectly, or confusing maximum of denominator with minimum of fraction.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(g(x) = \frac{1}{f(x)}\) and \(f(x) = x\), \(x \neq 0\), then which one of the following is correct?
questions.solution: With \(f(x)=x\) and \(g(x)=\frac{1}{x}\), note \(f(f(\cdots))=x\) always and \(g(g(x))=x\). Evaluating each composite: odd applications of \(g\) give \(\frac{1}{x}\), even give \(x\). Option D: LHS has 3 \(f\)'s and 2 \(g\)'s (net: \(\frac{1}{x}\)); RHS has 3 \(f\)'s and 1 \(g\) (net: \(\frac{1}{x}\)); both equal. Common mistake: losing track of even/odd composition counts.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The domain of the function \(f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{|x|-x}}\) is
questions.solution: For \(f\) to be defined, need \(|x|-x > 0\). If \(x\geq 0\): \(|x|=x\Rightarrow|x|-x=0\), so no solution. If \(x<0\): \(|x|=-x\Rightarrow|x|-x=-2x>0\). So domain is \((-\infty,0)\). Common mistake: including \(x=0\) or choosing \((-\infty,0]\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\), \(g:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be two functions given by \(f(x) = 2x-3\) and \(g(x) = x^{3}+5\), then \((f\circ g)^{-1}(x)\) is equal to
questions.solution: \((f\circ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = 2(x^{3}+5)-3 = 2x^{3}+7\). Let \(y=2x^{3}+7\Rightarrow x^{3}=\frac{y-7}{2}\Rightarrow x=\left(\frac{y-7}{2}\right)^{1/3}\). So \((f\circ g)^{-1}(x)=\left(\frac{x-7}{2}\right)^{1/3}\). Note: options A and D appear identical; correct form is \(\left(\frac{x-7}{2}\right)^{1/3}\). Common mistake: computing \(g^{-1}\circ f^{-1}\) incorrectly.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: \(f(xy) = f(x) + f(y)\) is true for all
questions.solution: The fundamental property of logarithms is \(\log(xy) = \log x + \log y\), which matches \(f(xy)=f(x)+f(y)\). This is the defining Cauchy functional equation satisfied by logarithms. Common mistake: thinking exponential functions satisfy this, when they satisfy \(f(x+y)=f(x)\cdot f(y)\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(f(x_1) - f(x_2) = f\!\left(\frac{x_1 - x_2}{1 - x_1 x_2}\right)\) for \(x_1, x_2 \in (-1,1)\), then what is \(f(x)\) equal to?
questions.solution: The argument \(\frac{x_1-x_2}{1-x_1 x_2}\) is the tanh-subtraction formula, suggesting \(f\) is related to \(\tanh^{-1}\). Indeed \(\tanh^{-1}(x)=\frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1+x}{1-x}\), so \(f(x)=\ln\frac{1-x}{1+x}\) satisfies the equation. Common mistake: confusing the tanh identity with the tan identity.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: What is the range of the function \(y = \frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\) where \(x \in \mathbb{R}\)?
questions.solution: Write \(y = 1 - \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\). Since \(x^{2}\geq 0\), we have \(1+x^{2}\geq 1\), so \(\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\in(0,1]\), giving \(y\in[0,1)\). At \(x=0\), \(y=0\); as \(x\to\infty\), \(y\to 1^{-}\). Common mistake: including 1 in the range, since \(y=1\) requires \(x=\infty\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(f(x)\) be the greatest integer function and \(g(x)\) be the modulus function. What is \((g\circ f)\!\left(-\frac{5}{3}\right) - (f\circ g)\!\left(-\frac{5}{3}\right)\) equal to?
questions.solution: \(f(-5/3)=\lfloor-5/3\rfloor=-2\), so \((g\circ f)(-5/3)=|-2|=2\). \(g(-5/3)=|-5/3|=5/3\), so \((f\circ g)(-5/3)=\lfloor 5/3\rfloor=1\). Thus \(2-1=1\)... result is 1. Common mistake: computing \(\lfloor-5/3\rfloor=-1\) instead of \(-2\) (floor rounds toward \(-\infty\)).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(f:A\to\mathbb{R}\) where \(A=\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}\) is such that \(f(x)=\frac{x+|x|}{x}\). On which one of the following sets is \(f(x)\) continuous?
questions.solution: For \(x>0\): \(f(x)=\frac{2x}{x}=2\). For \(x<0\): \(f(x)=\frac{x-x}{x}=0\). So \(f\) equals 0 on \((-\infty,0)\) and 2 on \((0,\infty)\), with a jump at 0. \(f\) is continuous on \(C=(-\infty,0)\subseteq A\) (where it's constantly 0). Common mistake: choosing \(A\) without noticing the jump discontinuity as \(x\to 0^+\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The function \(f(x) = |x| - x^{3}\) is
questions.solution: \(f(-x)=|-x|-(-x)^{3}=|x|+x^{3}\). Compare: \(f(x)=|x|-x^{3}\), \(-f(x)=-|x|+x^{3}\). Since \(f(-x)\neq f(x)\) and \(f(-x)\neq -f(x)\), the function is neither even nor odd. Common mistake: treating each term separately (\(|x|\) is even, \(x^{3}\) is odd) and incorrectly concluding the sum has a definite parity.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(f(x) = \frac{x}{x-1}\), then what is \(\frac{f(a)}{f(a+1)}\) equal to?
questions.solution: \(f(a)=\frac{a}{a-1}\), \(f(a+1)=\frac{a+1}{a}\). So \(\frac{f(a)}{f(a+1)}=\frac{a}{a-1}\cdot\frac{a}{a+1}=\frac{a^{2}}{a^{2}-1}\). Now \(f(a^{2})=\frac{a^{2}}{a^{2}-1}\). So the answer is \(f(a^{2})\). Common mistake: arithmetic errors when dividing the two fractions.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(f(x) = px+q\) and \(g(x) = mx+n\). Then \(f(g(x)) = g(f(x))\) is equivalent to
questions.solution: \(f(g(x))=p(mx+n)+q=pmx+pn+q\) and \(g(f(x))=m(px+q)+n=mpx+mq+n\). These are equal iff \(pn+q=mq+n\), i.e., \(pn+q=mq+n\). Check option C: \(f(n)=pn+q\) and \(g(q)=mq+n\). So \(f(n)=g(q)\) is exactly the required condition. Common mistake: equating coefficients of \(x\) (which gives \(pm=mp\), always true) and overlooking the constant term.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(f(x) = \begin{cases} x, & x \text{ is rational} \\ 0, & x \text{ is irrational} \end{cases}\) and \(g(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \text{ is rational} \\ x, & x \text{ is irrational} \end{cases}\). If \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) and \(g:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\), then \((f-g)\) is
questions.solution: \((f-g)(x)=x\) for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}\): if \(x\) is rational, \(f(x)-g(x)=x-0=x\); if irrational, \(0-x=-x\)... wait: \((f-g)(x)=x\) when rational and \(-x\) when irrational. Actually \((f-g)(x)=x\) for rational and \(0-x=-x\) for irrational — this is not the identity. Re-examining: \(f(x)-g(x)=x\) (rational) or \(-x\) (irrational). This is one-one and onto \(\mathbb{R}\). Common mistake: not realizing both branches together form a bijection onto \(\mathbb{R}\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(f(a) = \frac{a-1}{a+1}\). Consider: 1. \(f(2a) = f(a)+1\)  2. \(f\!\left(\frac{1}{a}\right) = -f(a)\). Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.solution: Statement 1: \(f(2a)=\frac{2a-1}{2a+1}\) and \(f(a)+1=\frac{a-1}{a+1}+1=\frac{2a}{a+1}\). These are not equal. Statement 2: \(f(1/a)=\frac{1/a-1}{1/a+1}=\frac{1-a}{1+a}=-\frac{a-1}{a+1}=-f(a)\) ✓. Common mistake: algebraic errors when computing \(f(1/a)\) or mistakenly verifying statement 1.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The inverse of the function \(y = 5^{\ln x}\) is
questions.solution: Take \(\ln\) of both sides: \(\ln y = \ln x \cdot \ln 5\), so \(\ln x = \frac{\ln y}{\ln 5}\), giving \(x = e^{\ln y/\ln 5} = y^{1/\ln 5}\). Since \(y=5^{\ln x}>0\), domain is \(y>0\). Common mistake: confusing \(\ln\) and \(\log_5\) manipulations.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(f(x) = \frac{\sqrt{x-1}}{x-4}\) defines a function on \(\mathbb{R}\), then what is its domain?
questions.solution:
Need \(x-1\geq 0\Rightarrow x\geq 1\) (for square root) and \(x-4
eq 0\Rightarrow x
eq 4\) (denominator). Combined: \(x\geq 1\) and \(x
eq 4\), giving \([1,4)\cup(4,\infty)\). Common mistake: using strict inequality for the square root (\(x>1\)) or forgetting to exclude \(x=4\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: A function \(f:A\to\mathbb{R}\) is defined by \(f(x)=x^{2}-4x+5\) where \(A=(1,4)\). What is the range of the function?
questions.solution: \(f(x)=(x-2)^{2}+1\) has vertex at \(x=2\), minimum value 1. On the open interval \((1,4)\): at \(x=2\), \(f=1\) (attained); as \(x\to 1^+\), \(f\to 2\); as \(x\to 4^-\), \(f\to 5\). Range is \([1,5)\). Common mistake: using closed interval \([1,5]\) by not recognizing the endpoints are excluded.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(f(x)=x^{2}\), \(g(x)=\tan x\) and \(h(x)=\ln x\). For \(x=\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\), what is the value of \([h\circ(g\circ f)](x)\)?
questions.solution: \((g\circ f)(x)=g(f(x))=\tan(x^{2})=\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=1\). Then \(h(1)=\ln 1=0\). Common mistake: computing \(f(g(x))\) instead of \(g(f(x))\), or evaluating at the wrong step.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(A=\{1,2,3,4,5\}\) and \(B=\{6,7\}\). What is the number of onto functions from \(A\) to \(B\)?
questions.solution: Total functions from \(A\) to \(B\): \(2^{5}=32\). Onto functions = total \(-\) functions missing at least one element of \(B\) = \(32 - \binom{2}{1}\cdot 1^{5} = 32-2=30\) (by inclusion-exclusion). Common mistake: using \(2^{5}=32\) without subtracting non-surjective functions.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: What is the domain of the function \(f(x) = 3^{x}\)?
questions.solution: Exponential functions \(a^{x}\) (with \(a>0, a\neq 1\)) are defined for all real \(x\). The domain is \(\mathbb{R}=(-\infty,\infty)\). Common mistake: confusing the domain of \(3^{x}\) (all reals) with the range (positive reals only).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: What is the domain of the function \(f(x) = \sqrt{1-(x-1)^{2}}\)?
questions.solution: Need \(1-(x-1)^{2}\geq 0\Rightarrow(x-1)^{2}\leq 1\Rightarrow|x-1|\leq 1\Rightarrow 0\leq x\leq 2\). Domain is \([0,2]\). Common mistake: solving the inequality as strict (\(<\)) giving open interval, or shifting incorrectly.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: What is the range of the function \(f(x)=1-\sin x\) defined on the entire real line?
questions.solution: \(\sin x\in[-1,1]\) for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}\), so \(1-\sin x\in[1-1,1-(-1)]=[0,2]\). Both endpoints are attained. Common mistake: choosing \((-1,1)\) by confusing the range of \(\sin x\) with the range of \(1-\sin x\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(f(x)\) be a polynomial function such that \(f\circ f(x) = x^{4}\). What is \(f'(1)\) equal to?
questions.solution: If \(f(f(x))=x^{4}\), try \(f(x)=x^{2}\): then \(f(f(x))=f(x^{2})=(x^{2})^{2}=x^{4}\) ✓. So \(f(x)=x^{2}\), giving \(f'(x)=2x\) and \(f'(1)=2\). Common mistake: trying more complicated polynomials or differentiating \(f(f(x))=x^4\) implicitly without first identifying \(f\).
